sentences.text: I forgot also to tell you that, while I was in Plaszow waiting until we can-- Schindler finishes the living quarters, and then I was working for the-- in a laundry ironing shirts for the Germans.
Well, it was a-- I wasn't too long there.
I was lucky that I wasn't too long there.
It was hectic.
It was terrible.
Not one time we came from work they didn't let us go-- we had to go to appellplatz, or somebody was missing, or somebody-- they were hanging somebody.
Or I remember, when they killed [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know what you heard.
He was the whole thing in-- in Plaszow.
We had to-- there were-- I don't know how many of them.
They killed three, four-- [INAUDIBLE] wife and some other--
And we had to-- we had to walk through and see how they lay.
And they-- and they told us that they got killed because they was-- wanted to escape.
I think shot.
You know?
I don't even know who killed-- Goth?
I'm sure that Goth, but--
Yes.
I saw him.
Not too-- thank God, not too-- too often, but I saw him.
I think he came even once to-- to Emalia where I was-- yeah.
He was tall and with the dog-- dog next to him.
And that's about-- that's about that.
It was nothing-- just barracks, just barracks and-- and bunk beds, one under-- under other.
No table, no chairs, nothing.
Just--
Yes.
Yeah.
But over there, it was very lenient.
The-- the-- after work, the men could come to the-- to the wives or to the girlfriend.
Or-- there was no dining room.
There was no-- I don't even remember where-- I think we were sitting on the-- on the bunk beds and-- and-- and eating.
But there was a doctor where you were not afraid that you-- when something was wrong with you, to go to doctor.
Because in Plaszow, you were afraid to go, because you never know.
There came a day they took out all the sick people from and they killed them.
And I was lucky that I didn't see too much of that.
I was not too long in-- and even if I was, I was always a quiet one.
I was always minding my-- my business.
And let the-- when they left me alone, I left everybody else alone.
And I obeyed, and that's how I survived.
Oh, very often.
He used to come to the factory.
First of all, do you did not have to see him, you can smell him.
You could-- in the minute he walked into the factory, you could smell him from perfume and cigars, smoking cigars.
I didn't realize at that time, but they say that he specially was lighting up every minute a cigar or cigarettes, and throwing on the floor.
And so our-- some of our people what they smoked, they could pick it up.
Yeah.
And I was telling you about my girlfriend.
She was-- she spoke perfect German.
She was from Berlin.
And she was kind of cute.
And he used to come-- I was working with her by one station.
He used to come and-- and a little bit like flirt with her.
It-- well, he could speak perfect-- you know, she could speak perfect German-- and have a conversation with her.
And she had a boyfriend in Kabul, so she asked him or he can bring him over.
And he-- he did.
But later on, didn't work out between them.
He went-- he went back.
And I thought that he-- oh, he always passed by.
He said, [GERMAN] you know, in-- in German.
He never touched nobody.
I never heard or saw for him to be, you know, cruel, or-- or-- And-- and I thought that he never pays attention to me.
But after the war, we lived in Regensburg.
And I was coming out of the street car, and he was supposed to go to a street car.
When he saw me, he started to wave.
And he recognized me.
And he kissed me.
And he asked me how I am doing.
And he recognized me.
He talked to me.
But I kept-- it's a funny thing.
I still didn't-- it was too early.
I-- I didn't feel free with him, just to speak.
I was still like the-- the-- I was still, like, afraid.
He still was a-- he was a German.
I guess so.
Yeah, I guess so.
So-- well, of course, I didn't know all the good things he did for other, you know, people, because the book what is written, I read stories about him, but I never knew that it-- that's what it was.
And like I-- they were interviewing me here.
And like I said, he was a human being.
First, he did it for himself.
And then the humanity kicked-- kicked in.
And he knew also, to the-- later on, that the Germans are losing the-- the war.
And that's why he was alive.
Because in Brinnlitz-- we went later on to Brinnlitz-- our people got him out.
Otherwise, the Russians-- because the Russians liberated us--
Oh.
Well--
Well, that's-- that's-- that's the-- another story.
We-- then it came out the-- well, we were in Plaszow-- came out the list.
A lot of people, what they supposed to be there-- there was a guy by the name Marcel Goldberg.
And he was the one who had the power over that-- that list.
Well, of course, Schindler.
And there was-- Schindler had a-- a guy, whose name was Behnke.
In the movie, they describe it's the-- the-- his bookkeeper.
But Behnke was really the owner of the factory before the war.
You never saw Schindler at the factory, without him next.
It was really funny, because Schindler was tall and handsome, and he was short and fat.
Well anyhow, but if you wanted something from Schindler, first, you had to go to Behnke.
And then, Behnke talked to-- to-- to Schindler.
And then came the list.
So a lot of people were-- the ones who really worked for him, were taken out.
And Marcel Goldberg was taking diamonds and money and putting--
To get on the list.
My brother came on the list too.
I was later on with my brother and my sister-in-law.
And this-- it-- everybody tried to save himself.
So my brother, whatever he had, he gave him.
And he was on the list.
And my sister-in-law was on the list.
And my brother said that he took care of for me too.
But I was like double, because I was one of the first one working there.
So my sister's supposed to be-- Ernestina, Erna, she's supposed to be there, but she was not.
But her-- her husband was, but that was just a coincidence.
Anyhow-- anyhow-- and-- and then, and-- they said that we're going-- he built Brinnlitz, another factory in Czechoslovakia.
So that we're supposed to go straight there.
Meanwhile, the 300 women went to Auschwitz.
What happened when we got there?
We were just there.
They put us in the barracks.
And we were just laying there, going, standing for hours in the rain.
At that time, it was raining and muddy.
And they were giving us some soup like water and bread.
And we were waiting and waiting.
We didn't know what-- One thing, they didn't put the numbers on us.
So usually-- but at that time, they say that they already didn't put into the oven.
That was in-- in '44 already.
Yeah.
Nothing.
You-- you put yourself-- did you see the movie, like in the shower?
I was there.
And we didn't know.
Today, we are here.
Tomorrow, we might not be.
And there was nothing-- we were going like cattles, you know, to the barn.
We didn't--
In-- in Auschwitz?
Three weeks.
Three weeks, because I didn't know at that time, Schindler came for us.
He said those people supposed to go straight to-- to Brinnlitz.
And like I found out later on that he gave-- he bribed them.
He gave them money to get us out.
And the men were sent Gross-Rosen.
They supposed to go also straight to Brinnlitz.
So that's what he did.
He took us out from-- from Auschwitz.
I remember, while we were going already from Auschwitz to the trains-- you know, they-- those were the like cattles-- cattle-- what-- how you call it?
Cattle cars.
Cattle cars.
And my sister was going also out of Auschwitz.
That's-- I was so happy about it.
But she was going to one side of the train, and I was going to the other.
And I remember we were waving to each other.
And she was screaming my name, and I was screaming her name.
And-- but I was relieved that I knew that she's leaving Auschwitz.
I didn't know where.
Later on, I found out they took her to Lichtenvoorde to a factory.
But she went through hell over there.
It was very, very-- you know, much worser than we had in-- in Brinnlitz, because the whole Brinnlitz was like a setup.
There was no production made over there.
Schindler just put this together, I guess, to save us.
When I went to--
Well, we were, again, in those cattle.
We didn't know where we are going.
And then we came to Brinnlitz.
We came to Brinnlitz.
There was not even-- the living quarters were not even-- the-- the bunk beds were not even made, so we were sleeping just down on the floor, until they-- they build it.
And we worked.
Every day, we worked.
We got some beet soup.
We were lucky.
We got some soup with horse meat in it.
And at that time, I worked with my brother and with my sister-in-law in the factory.
My brother think-- the whole time during the war, he didn't know what means hunger, really, even in Plaszow.
And that was the first time, in Brinnlitz, what he was lacking of food.
And he was getting-- just like his eyes were wild from-- from hunger.
And I remember when we were getting that soup with the horse meat, to take out that meat out of the-- put on a piece of a paper.
And little piece of bread, what I got, I sliced it thin as I could.
And I made few sandwiches with that meat.
And I was eating one for here then, and then for later.
And when we were having like a lunch break, or in the evening a break, my brother didn't have nothing.
I still had mine.
And what do you think?
I didn't give it to him?
He was-- my sister-in-law too.
And he was fighting.
He didn't want to, but he-- I made him take it, because he-- he didn't really take it good.
He was-- really, that was a-- a-- a blow to him from-- you know, I-- we gradually-- well, but he had everything in ghetto.
I mean, in Plaszow and in ghetto.
So that's--
At Brinnlitz?
Not too much.
There was a-- I was filing some-- they're from the ammunition, the things.
But there was no work.
We were doing this over and over, the same things.
That was already to the end.
It was end of '44 and beginning of '45.
Oh, yeah.
They used to come.
There was one guy whose name was Leopold-- Leo-- I don't remember his name.
And of course, we-- he was going to the factory to get with Schindler.
And we all were pretending, you know?
And-- and I also heard over there that they were already digging-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --that they were already digging holes for us to-- to, you know.
That they will kill us, put us in the hole, and-- but then, Schindler, he took few guys and he gave them ammunition, you know, rifles.
And he says, when it will come-- it's already bad.
And it would comes to that, you, whoever can save himself, you save.
They were guys from Budzyn.
They came from-- from Budzyn.
Yeah.
And [INAUDIBLE] knew about it, too.
I did not know those things.
All those things I found out later.
And what-- we needed to go to the bathroom, there was a woman watching us all the time.
We couldn't go separate to the bathroom.
We had to wait till the whole group had to go, and go in-- in groups.
And that's how we-- one day, we-- we get up, and the Russians-- no, when it was too close, Schindler had a speech to us.
To tell you the truth, I don't-- in the beginning-- but then I recall something that I-- it was so many people, I hardly could even hear what he was saying.
But-- but he gave a speech, yeah.
And then, our people helped him to escape.
No.
No, I did not see him escape.
No.
Oh, one more thing.
Like, in Brinnlitz, there were guys working in the kitchen.
And there were guys working outside.
And they were bringing, like, wool to knit from outside from someplace.
And it was cold, so they were giving us.
My sister-in-law met there a guy what he worked in the kitchen.
And he gave her the wool.
And we were knitting for them the sweaters.
And he was giving us extra soup, extra bread.
And that way, we-- we survived.
As I remember, I was sitting up sometimes till 1:00 in the morning and knitting, yeah, together with my sister-in-law.
What happened?
I remember that I was with my brother and my sister-in-law.

And there-- when Schindler left, there was the food in the-- in the warehouses, and fabrics, and-- So a committee got-- some people got together, and they went and they divide a little for everybody, whatever it was left, and some food.
And I remember my brother-- we got some cream of wheat-- and he made a fire outside.
And he cooked for us the cream of wheat.
And we didn't stay too long.
And we started to hitchhike back to-- to Poland, to Krakow, to see or anybody.
We-- we were still hoping that-- that maybe somebody survived.
I remember, when I was in ghetto, after they-- they took my mother, I was there at that time with my sister when she was married.
We used to go at night, because there was no bathrooms inside.
We had to go outside with a candle.
And we used to say, we thought that-- they-- they took my mother to work someplace.
I said, I hope mother is not cold.
We were both crying and saying, I hope mother is OK, she's not-- she's not cold.
Meanwhile, she wasn't alive anymore.
And then, we-- we came-- we hitchhiked to Prague.
And whatever they-- people were taking us.
And-- and-- and-- and-- when they were traveling with horses and buggy or-- or-- or-- not cars, but trucks.
And we got back to Krakow.
And there was the Jewish Community Center there already.
And first, we went there to see are we-- with us was going a-- a fellow.
I forgot his name, but he was a Jewish policeman.
And he even was making passes at me.
But in a minute he came to the Jewish Community Center, my brother told me they took him, they put him to jail.
They killed him.
I don't know what he did.
He must have done something wrong.
And we didn't know what to do.
We didn't know where to go.
My sister-in-law comes from a well-to-do family before the war.
The parents own two big, big houses, apartment houses there.
One was on the street of [POLISH].
They changed to I don't remember what, because when I was a couple of years ago in Poland, I went there.
And one was on [POLISH].
And right away, she started to-- she was trying to get a place to live in her own-- you know, it was hers, the parents.
Her father passed away before the war and her mother during the war.
Anyhow, so we went to one of the homes now on [POLISH].
And the-- the janitor knew her.
And she let us in.
And we slept in her kitchen, the three of us, on the floor, for quite some times, until she got into her other building.
She got a permission to get-- we got at that time, like, like [POLISH].
It was a-- a kitchen, and a bedroom, and a-- a bathroom, you know.
Inside, it was a mother and a thing, so we slept on the floor until she-- we got into the-- to that place.
And my brother, whoever he-- whoever he met, if he didn't have where to-- to-- to live, he was bringing him.
They were sleeping on the floor in our place, people, after the war.
And that's how I met my husband.
Because I had a cousin.
He's not alive.
He passed away-- he was 46 years old-- of cancer.
And he was like a brother with him in Mauthausen, because my husband was in Mauthausen, Gusen I, Gusen II.
And he-- they both saved each other's life.
And after the war, my cousin, of course, was my brother's cousin, too.
We-- we met him.
And my brother says to my cousin, you come.
There was not too many relatives left.
If you found a cousin, you were happy.
You come and live with me in my place.
So he says, I can't, because I have a friend what he saved my life.
And I can't leave him.
So he says, out of the kitchen, where we had the place, was like a place like for a maid, made just a room for a-- for a bed.
He says, instead of to sleep there alone, you will sleep with your friend.
And that was my husband.
What's your husband's name?
Stanley.
Stan.
Yes.
Yeah.
No, I was guarded with my-- my brother was all the time.
I was guarded by my brother and my sister-in-law.
I wasn't by myself.
There was no occasion to-- I was scared.
I remember when we were sitting once, my brother was making-- like cooking the cream of wheat, one passed by.
They looked, but they were-- they didn't do any harm to me.
Some girls say that they were trying to-- you know, to rape them or whatever.
So we lived there in the-- well, it was hard in the beginning, because we had to start from scratch.
My brother took things what we got.
I got some things, they were like jackets.
And he had.
And-- and they gave us two, three each.
And he went in a place there in Krakow, and he sold it.
And then he started little by little.
But the beginning was very bad.
That's all what we ate is bread and potatoes.
And I even went to the Jewish Community Center.
It killed me, but I went with a thing for soup, until my brother started to make some business.
This brother is the-- that's the brother, he lives now in Australia, in Melbourne.
His name is Herman.
And he saved my life, really.
Like I said in the-- in the book  there, that before Schindler saved me, he saved me.
Because if he wouldn't take us out from Wieliczka at that time, none of us would survive.
Right after the-- after the war, because we still were hoping to see, when we came to Krakow.
First thing, we went to the house there where we lived.
And we had a janitor what-- there what we were friendly with her.
And-- and when she saw me, and she said, oh, my god.
From all of them, you lived through?
You're such a quiet one.
It was meant to be.
It was-- I guess it was meant to be.
And your sister, who was taken away from Auschwitz in the other direction, what happened to her?
From Auschwitz-- oh yeah, she-- she's OK.
She-- after the-- when we came to Krakow, I found out that she's-- she's already there.
And she had that little girl.
Or my little-- the little girl-- I forgot to tell you that, in ghetto, before-- before they took my mother, when I went for that night shift, my brother arranged.
And he took out the-- handed the girl to a Gentile family.
Yeah.
And she lived through.
She lives now.
She's over 50 years old now.
She lives in Camarillo.
Unfortunately, she's sick, but-- but she lived through.
And-- and then, my sister got together-- her husband lived through too, because he was with us in Brinnlitz.
And then, in 1946, I-- after six months knowing my husband, we got married, and--
In Krakow.
That was in my sister's house, that I gave you the-- some-- some picture.
And after six months, there started to be already pogroms, believe it or not.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Well, I-- I wasn't present, but my brother came.
And he says, I--
